title: Feels sus / scammy
review:
This review is based only on the sign-up, I never actually made it into the app. Two huge red flags:

1) Despite multiple opportunities to say how much the app subscription costs, it does  not indicate this anywhere. You’re expected to agree to a 5-day trial and then be charged through the app store. My assumption is they’re angling for maximizing first-month revenue. 

2) The sign-up process feels like every other self-help app: it starts out with perfunctory questions to prime you to imagine how it might help you and then rather than letting you try out the app first, they immediately convert you to a trial subscription (see above). In my experience with other apps, these initial questions are not conveyed on to the app, so the point of the questions is purely to convince you to convert to a subscriber, and not to actually be used for your benefit. (Having not used this app, I dont know for certain this is the case — but it has all the hallmarks of these poor quality self help apps)

Install the app, if you want, you’ll see the issues I mentioned right away.

title: Update looks nice, but feels dumbed down
review:
The update looks nice, but feels dumbed down. All the content has been replaced and all user progress lost. There are fewer lessons now and fewer categories to choose from. The questions and answers often feel too easy now, and the AI art is distracting. Ive used it a lot the last couple weeks and given the update a chance. I think it has lost a lot of value it once had. 

The app itself teaches about how we are more deeply affected by loss than we are uplifted by an equivalent gain. Im sure a lot of the user base was frustrated by the sudden loss of all the old content and progress. Can you bring any of it back?

So I was scrolling on TikTok and I’ve seen a few videos abt his app they was talkin abt how this changed a cousin of theirs from the shy introvert to the confident extrovert and I was like I need this all they said was this app l JUST like Duolingo key word  just  so I look this up on the AppStore and I look at the recommend ages and it says 12+ and I’m like ok this is good cause I ain’t 18 and got no money then I go into the review’s and one of them talkin abt how you need to pay a subscription to use the app and I’m like wth cause u gon let a twelve year old on here when theirs a subscription we’ll talk abt that more later then I see the developer respond sayin that they fixed it and users don’t have to get a subscription to use it  so I download it and I creat and profile or whatever then I see sum to sign up for a subscription and I’m like what I thought u didn’t have to do so I’m lookin for the little x to show up cause like they said we ain’t have to…. It ain’t show up. And I leave the app and go back in and it let me  do one round jus one ☝️ and I’m like why they letting 12yr olds on here if theirs a subscription but then I remember it asked for my age and it started at 18 not 12 18 and I’m like I thought it was recommended for people 12 and older but I guess it’s not. Imagine a 12 yr old got this subscription thinking they would cancel the subscription and using they mummy credit card then they don’t causing problems so if ur gonna have a required subscription and recommend this app for ages 12+ jus don’t do it.

title: Frustrating paywall before you can even try it
review:
I was really excited to try Gleam after hearing good things, but the sign-up process immediately killed my interest. Right from the start, it pushes you to subscribe before you’ve seen anything meaningful in the app. Yes, they offer a free trial, but it still requires entering payment info and enabling a recurring subscription that I’d have to remember to cancel.
I get that developers need to make money, but this approach feels like a money grab. If you truly believe in your product and want people to fall in love with it, why not let users explore the core features freely first—without tying anything to a recurring billing cycle? Removing that pressure would make a huge difference. I know I would have happily signed up and given it a real shot if I could try it risk-free without the subscription commitment looming.
As it stands, I closed the app and deleted it without ever getting past the sign-up screen. Disappointing.

title: Oof
review:
$70 a year is laughably expensive for the most generic advice available. Advice that is actively harmful to neurodivergent people by encouraging them to Mask in order to preserve the comfort of everyone else. It’s exhausting and often makes the neurodivergent individual feel just as lonely as they were before because they’re “not allowed” to be themselves.
Instead of demanding “forced genuine” smiles and friendliness, maybe we can shift our focus to educating normies about differences in cognition and communication styles and start, you know… actually accommodating for literal disabilities? Just a thought.

title: $70 to tell you to “force a genuine smile”
review:
I don’t know why reviews don’t just start with “it is $70” Duolingo for being manipulative, basically. How am I to “force” a “genuine” smile?

Here’s the advice in a nutshell: smile, act happy, don’t think bad things, don’t criticize people or be negative in front of them, say appreciative things.

It’s the vibe-coded app version of Dale Carnegie’s book. Has formatting issues. Typos.

I also would argue some of the advice given is genuinely poor and dated. The barista Chloe does not want you to address her by name because you read her nametag. Only “ownership” class people think that way. Hourly service workers generally find it invasive when customers speak with intense familiarity immediately. This is crackpot stuff designed to trick lonely or dysfunctional people into losing $70. 

I also really enjoyed all the reminders in the lessons that other people don’t care about you, don’t want to hear what you have to say, don’t care if they hurt you and you couldn’t even try to tell them because others will not listen as they  are inherently selfish. 

The advice is geared towards middle management types trying to get ahead in careers where promotion is based on social engineering. It’s not going to teach you how to build up real friends or relationships, just how to have dinner with other vacuous soul-suckers in the never ending pursuit of money. Don’t believe me? Just wait till you get to the lesson about the CEO and his million dollar check! 🤮

The “ai” aspect lets you write completely insane responses and accepts them as valid. It accepted “what is your favorite oat to grow” as an ice breaking question.

title: My Social Anxiety has Improved So Much
review: The way I’d describe my social anxiety— it’s like test anxiety where you are so nervous you forget all of the answers, except it’s every time I have a social interaction outside of immediate family and I completely forget all of the words I know. I have panic attacks leaving the house  even just going to the grocery store. I avoid leaving the house altogether for the most part. It’s like I’m stuck and I just can’t get myself out. I tried everything! Counseling, books, alternative therapies, medication, herbs etc.  Some helped a little bit, most did not but this app made the most improvement by far! Now I know the mechanics of my social anxiety. Ive stoped avoiding situations that scare me as much. I’ve started to leave my house a lot more often. I’ve started to say more in conversations with strangers— sometimes I don’t even think about it. My brain doesn’t blank out as much when it comes to conversing. I still have a long way to go but I wanted to say thank you to the people who made this app. It’s changed my life.

Although I still have long ways to go, what this app has done so far is amazing!
